title: Ternary expressions

overview.what:
A ternary expression (also called a conditional expression) evaluates to one of two values based on a condition, all in a single line:

  value_if_true if condition else value_if_false

Examples:

  label = 'admin' if user['role'] == 'superuser' else 'member'
  display = name if name else 'Anonymous'
  status_text = 'enabled' if config.get('debug') else 'disabled'

The expression evaluates the condition first, then returns exactly ONE of the two branches — the other is never evaluated. It is an expression, not a statement, so it can appear anywhere a value is expected: assignment, function argument, f-string, list element. Think of it as a compressed two-branch decision that returns a value — not a replacement for multi-branch logic.
overview.why: Ternary expressions compress routine two-outcome decisions (present vs missing, enabled vs disabled, role A vs role B) into one readable line. They become unreadable when nested or when the condition or branches are complex — those cases still call for a full if/else block.

why_learning_this: Compressing simple two-branch assignments in FastAPI serializers and Pydantic models to a single readable line
Reading library code and open-source projects that use conditional expressions extensively

common_mistakes.title: Nesting ternary expressions
common_mistakes.explanation: a if x else b if y else c is legal but hard to read. Replace with a full if/elif/else block when there are more than two outcomes.

common_mistakes.title: Confusing the syntax order
common_mistakes.explanation: Python's order is value_if_true if condition else value_if_false — the opposite of C's `condition ? true : false`. Write it as 'give me X, IF this is true, ELSE Y'.

common_mistakes.title: Using a ternary when the branches have side effects
common_mistakes.explanation: Ternary expressions are for selecting values, not for branching on actions. If either branch calls a function for its side effect, a full if/else block is clearer.

recall_questions.q: What is the syntax of a Python ternary expression?
When the condition is False, which branch is evaluated — the true branch or the false branch?
recall_questions.answer: Only the false branch (after else) is evaluated; the true branch is skipped entirely.

recall_questions.q: When should you prefer a full if/else block over a ternary expression?
recall_questions.answer: When there are more than two outcomes, when the branches have side effects, or when the expression would need to be nested — all of which hurt readability.

practice_tasks.title: Role label formatter
practice_tasks.prompt: Write format_role(user) that returns 'Admin (<name>)' when user['role'] == 'admin' and 'User (<name>)' otherwise, using a ternary expression. Test with an admin and a regular user.
practice_tasks.solution:
def format_role(user):
    prefix = 'Admin' if user['role'] == 'admin' else 'User'
    return f'{prefix} ({user["name"]})'

print(format_role({'name': 'alice', 'role': 'admin'}))
print(format_role({'name': 'bob', 'role': 'member'}))

code_walkthrough.focus: The ternary evaluates the condition (role == 'admin') and returns exactly one of the two string values — the other branch is never touched.

challenge.title: Response status line builder
challenge.prompt: Write status_line(code, message=None) that returns a formatted string. Use a ternary so that when message is provided it reads '<code> <message>' and when it is None it reads '<code> (no message)'. Test with (200, 'OK'), (404, 'Not Found'), and (500, None).
challenge.solution:
def status_line(code, message=None):
    body = message if message is not None else '(no message)'
    return f'{code} {body}'

print(status_line(200, 'OK'))
print(status_line(404, 'Not Found'))
print(status_line(500))
